title: Working with NA and NULL
description: Understand missing values (NA) and empty objects (NULL).

slides.title: NA: Missing Data ❓
slides.content:
`NA` stands for **Not Available**. It represents missing or undefined data. Many real-world datasets contain missing values.

```r
x <- c(1, 2, NA, 4)
```

Operations involving NA usually return NA, unless explicitly handled.

```r
mean(x)  # returns NA
```

To check for NA, use `is.na()`:

```r
is.na(x)  # FALSE FALSE TRUE FALSE
```

slides.title: Working with NA
slides.codeSnippet:
v <- c(5, NA, 10)
print(v * 2)
print(sum(v))
print(is.na(v))

quizData.question: What is the result of `NA == NA`?
quizData.explanation: You cannot compare missing values; any operation with NA typically returns NA.

slides.title: NULL: Empty Object 🕳️
slides.content:
`NULL` represents the absence of an object or a vector of zero length. It is often used to indicate that a value is undefined or empty.

```r
x <- NULL
```

Unlike NA, NULL is not a value within a vector; it's the vector itself being empty.

```r
length(NULL)   # 0
is.null(x)     # TRUE
```

You can use `NULL` to remove elements from lists.

slides.title: NULL vs NA
slides.codeSnippet:
na_vec <- c(1, NA, 3)
null_vec <- NULL

print(length(na_vec))   # 3
print(length(null_vec)) # 0

# Removing NA elements
clean_vec <- na_vec[!is.na(na_vec)]
print(clean_vec)

# NULL can be used to delete list elements
lst <- list(a=1, b=2)
lst$a <- NULL
print(lst)

quizData.question: What is the length of `c(1, NULL, 3)`?
quizData.explanation: NULL disappears when combined with other elements in a vector, so it's like it was never there. The result is `c(1, 3)` of length 2.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
Many functions have an `na.rm` argument to ignore NA values:

```r
mean(c(1, NA, 3), na.rm = TRUE)  # 2
```

Always consider how you want to handle missing data in your analysis.

slides.title: Handle NA
slides.content: Calculate the sum of `c(10, NA, 20, NA, 30)` while ignoring NAs.
slides.codeSnippet:
data <- c(10, NA, 20, NA, 30)
sum_data <- sum(data, na.rm = TRUE)
print(sum_data)
